mark_scheme.question: Describe the natural topography of the northern mountains.
mark_scheme_points: High altitude peaks usually snow-covered (above 4500m)
Steep-sided valleys, narrow and V-shaped
Rugged landscape with deep gorges
Presence of glaciers in higher regions
Fast-flowing rivers forming waterfalls and rapids
Bare rocks due to lack of vegetation at high altitudes
examiner_tips: Award 1 mark per valid descriptive point.
Avoid referring to climate or vegetation; focus strictly on topography (physical shape).

mark_scheme.question: Explain the importance of the northern mountains to Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Source of major rivers (Indus and tributaries) from melting snow and glaciers
Provide water for irrigation and domestic use throughout the year
Hydroelectric power generation (HEP) potential in steep valleys with fast-flowing water
Acts as a barrier against cold winds from Central Asia in winter
Timber from coniferous forests provides raw material for construction and furniture
Provides a natural border and defence barrier in the north
Scenic beauty attracts domestic and international tourism, providing income and employment
examiner_tips: Ensure points are developed (e.g., 'source of rivers' + 'provides water for irrigation').

mark_scheme.question: Describe how the karez system of irrigation operates.
mark_scheme_points: An underground canal or tunnel system used in Balochistan
Taps into groundwater at the base of hills/mountains
Water flows by gravity down a gentle slope to the fields
Vertical shafts are dug at intervals for cleaning, maintenance, and ventilation
Reduces water loss through evaporation because the water flows underground
examiner_tips: Reference to 'underground', 'gravity flow', and 'vertical shafts' are key points for marks.

mark_scheme.question: Explain the causes of floods in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Heavy monsoon rainfall in July and August causing rivers to overflow
Rapid melting of snow and glaciers in the northern mountains due to rising summer temperatures
Deforestation in the catchment areas reducing the soil's ability to hold water, leading to rapid surface runoff
Siltation in rivers and dams reduces their water-holding capacity
Failure/breaching of embankments, dams, and barrages under water pressure
Cyclones in the Arabian Sea bringing torrential rains to coastal areas

mark_scheme.question: Evaluate the extent to which floods can be managed. Give reasons to support your judgement.
mark_scheme_points: To a great extent: Building multi-purpose dams (e.g., Tarbela, Mangla) helps hold back excess water.
To a great extent: Constructing stronger embankments and levees along river banks prevents overflow.
To a great extent: Afforestation/reforestation in foothills reduces surface runoff and prevents soil erosion.
To a great extent: Advanced early warning systems and weather forecasting (dams telemetry) allow timely evacuations.
To a limited extent: The cost of building and maintaining large dams and infrastructure is too high for a developing country like Pakistan.
To a limited extent: Siltation continuously reduces the effectiveness of existing reservoirs.
To a limited extent: Climate change is causing unprecedented and unpredictable rainfall patterns that overwhelm defences (e.g., 2022 super floods).
examiner_tips: Answers must provide arguments for both 'can be managed' and 'difficult to manage' to reach higher marks. Include a clear conclusion.

mark_scheme.question: Describe the distribution of coal in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Mainly found in Balochistan (Quetta, Mach, Sore Range, Khost, Harnai)
Large reserves recently developed in Sindh (Thar coalfield, Lakhra)
Also found in Punjab (Salt Range, Makarwal)
Found in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (Hangu)

mark_scheme.question: Explain why mostly imported coal is used for power generation instead of local coal.
mark_scheme_points: Local coal is mainly lignite or sub-bituminous, which is of low quality
Local coal has a low heating value/calorific value, creating less energy when burned
It has high ash and moisture content
It has high sulphur content, causing more air pollution and acid rain when burnt
Mining local coal is difficult due to thin seams and dangerous geological conditions (gas explosions)
Lack of capital/investment and modern technology to extract coal efficiently
Transportation of coal from remote areas (e.g., Thar) to power stations is expensive and undeveloped

mark_scheme.question: Describe the main features of the Lower Indus Plain.
mark_scheme_points: Extends from Mithankot to the Arabian Sea (covers most of Sindh)
Flat, gently undulating land
Features the Indus river flowing as a single immense river
Active flood plain alongside the river, subject to annual flooding
Presence of oxbow lakes and meanders
Presence of limestone ridges (cuestas) such as the Rohri and Ganjo Takkar
Ends in the Indus Delta, featuring distributaries, swamps, and mangrove forests

mark_scheme.question: Describe the types of forests found in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Coniferous forests: found in northern and north-western mountains; evergreen, cone-bearing trees like deodar, pine.
Alpine forests: found at very high altitudes; stunted growth, mainly shrubs.
Sub-tropical dry forests / scrub: found in foothills, Potohar plateau; thorny and drought-resistant bushes.
Tropical thorn forests (Rakh processing): found in Punjab and Sindh plains; deep roots, thorny trees like Acacia, Babool.
Mangrove forests: found in the Indus and Hub deltas/coastal areas; tolerate salt water, have aerial roots.
Riverine / Bela forests: found along the banks of the Indus and its tributaries; depend on inundation.

mark_scheme.question: Explain the causes of deforestation in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Cutting wood for domestic fuel / firewood, especially in rural areas lacking gas supply
Commercial logging for timber used in construction and furniture making
Clearing land for agriculture and farming to feed a growing population
Overgrazing by livestock (goats, sheep) preventing the growth of saplings/new shoots
Clearing land for urbanization, housing schemes, and infrastructure (roads, dams)
Illegal logging / timber mafia operating in northern areas without government checks

mark_scheme.question: Evaluate the effects of deforestation on the economy and environment.
mark_scheme_points: Environmental effect: Loss of habitat and biodiversity, endangering wildlife species.
Environmental effect: Increased soil erosion because tree roots no longer hold the soil, leading to barren land.
Environmental effect: Less transpiration causes reduced rainfall, altering local microclimates (climate change).
Economic effect: Increased surface runoff causes massive flooding, destroying crops, infrastructure, and human life.
Economic effect: Siltation of reservoirs reduces their lifespan, affecting HEP generation and irrigation water capacity.
Economic effect: Shortage of timber raw material for the furniture and sports goods industry, increasing expensive imports.
Economic effect: Loss of scenic beauty decreases tourism revenue in mountainous areas.

mark_scheme.question: State the names of three main cash crops grown in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Cotton
Sugarcane
Tobacco
Rice (Basmati is a major export)

mark_scheme.question: Explain the geographical requirements for growing cotton.
mark_scheme_points: Requires high temperatures around 25°C to 35°C during the growing season
Needs plenty of sunshine / dry days especially during the ripening and picking period
Moderate to low rainfall needed (no rain during picking to prevent damage to the lint)
Needs reliable irrigation to supplement rainfall
Requires alluvial soil/loam which retains moisture well but is drained

mark_scheme.question: Evaluate the problems faced by farmers in increasing agricultural production.
mark_scheme_points: Water shortages and unequal distribution of canal water; reliance on unpredictable rainfall.
Waterlogging and salinity reducing the fertility of the soil.
Lack of capital/funds prevents farmers from buying modern machinery (tractors) and high-yield variety (HYV) seeds.
Illiteracy/lack of education among farmers, making them hesitant to adopt modern, scientific farming methods.
Land fragmentation (small, scattered landholdings) due to inheritance laws makes the use of heavy machinery impractical.
Pests, diseases, and locust attacks ruin large portions of the crop harvest.
Poor infrastructure (unpaved farm-to-market roads) causing crop spoilage before reaching markets.
examiner_tips: Both natural/physical and human/economic problems should be covered.

mark_scheme.question: Describe the main features of the railway network in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Network is most dense in the Punjab and Sindh provinces
The main line runs roughly north-south from Karachi through Lahore to Peshawar
Sparse network in Balochistan, mainly linking Quetta to the rest of the country and Iran
Virtually no railway network in the northern mountainous regions / Gilgit-Baltistan
Mostly broad-gauge tracks, with some meter/narrow gauge lines in remote areas
Single-track lines are common, though the main line is double-tracked in major sections

mark_scheme.question: Explain the advantages of transporting goods by pipeline rather than by road.
mark_scheme_points: Pipelines provide continuous, uninterrupted flow of goods (oil/gas) 24/7
Cheaper to operate in the long term compared to running fleets of trucks/tankers
Safer method for hazardous materials, reducing the risk of road accidents and spillages
Not affected by weather conditions (floods, fog, landslides) which block roads
Reduces traffic congestion on national highways
Environmentally cleaner during operation compared to exhaust fumes from diesel trucks
Less vulnerable to theft/hijacking compared to road tankers

mark_scheme.question: Describe the distribution of population in Sindh.
mark_scheme_points: Highly irregular and unevenly distributed
Very high population density in Karachi, spreading out along the urban coast
High density along the course of the River Indus due to fertile soil and water availability
High population in urban centers like Hyderabad, Sukkur, and Larkana
Very low density/sparse population in the Tharparkar desert region in the east
Low density in the Kohistan / Kirthar mountain regions in the west

mark_scheme.question: Explain the factors that have caused a high birth rate in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Lack of education/illiteracy, especially among females, leads to earlier marriages and less awareness of family planning
Religious and cultural beliefs opposing the use of contraceptives
Preference for male children means families keep having children until a son is born
High infant mortality rate encourages couples to have more children to ensure some survive to adulthood
In rural agricultural areas, children are seen as free labor for the farm
Lack of government funding and accessibility for widespread family planning clinics, especially in remote areas
Tradition of early, often arranged, marriages extending the reproductive lifespan of women

mark_scheme.question: Describe the characteristics of the Mangrove forests.
mark_scheme_points: Found in coastal, saline/saltwater environments (Indus and Hub delta)
They have pneumatophores (aerial roots) which stick out of the mud to take in oxygen
They have broad, leathery leaves to reduce transpiration
Survive in waterlogged, muddy soil in tidal zones
They are broad-leaved evergreens
Trees are generally low in height, forming dense thickets

mark_scheme.question: Explain the economic and environmental importance of Mangrove forests.
mark_scheme_points: Economic: Provide a breeding ground for fish and shrimp, supporting the commercial fishing industry/exports.
Economic: Provide a source of timber for fuel wood, boat building, and camel fodder for local coastal communities.
Environmental: Protect the coastline from erosion caused by sea waves and storm surges.
Environmental: Act as a natural barrier/shield against cyclones and tsunamis.
Environmental: Provide a unique habitat for coastal wildlife and migratory birds.
Environmental: Absorb large amounts of carbon dioxide, helping to mitigate climate change.

mark_scheme.question: Explain what is meant by 'small-scale industry'.
mark_scheme_points: Industries that employ a small number of workers (usually less than 50)
Require low capital investment to set up and run
Often rely on local raw materials and manual labor/simple machinery
Examples include sports goods making, surgical instruments, carpet weaving, pottery

mark_scheme.question: Evaluate the importance of the sports goods and surgical instruments industries to Pakistan's economy.
mark_scheme_points: Positive: Major source of foreign exchange earnings through exports (Sialkot is world famous).
Positive: Highly labor-intensive, providing employment to millions, reducing poverty and rural-urban migration.
Positive: Empowers local craftsmen and utilizes local raw materials (e.g., leather, steel).
Positive: Creates a strong international reputation for Pakistani manufacturing (e.g., FIFA footballs).
Limitation: Highly vulnerable to child labor controversies, leading to boycotts by international brands.
Limitation: Relies on manual skills, so unable to scale production as rapidly as automated mega-factories in countries like China.
Limitation: Lack of modern technology and R&D keeps these industries at risk of becoming outdated.
examiner_tips: Provide both the positive economic contributions and the limitations or challenges associated with these industries.

mark_scheme.question: Describe the layout of a typical karez irrigation system.
mark_scheme_points: Starts from groundwater source in hills
Water drawn through underground tunnel
Vertical shafts for maintenance/air
Water flows by gravity
Emerges at surface/fields

mark_scheme.question: Evaluate the success of agricultural extension programmes in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Success: Introduction of HYV seeds increased yields
Success: Education on fertilizers and pesticides reduced crop diseases
Failure: Illiteracy among farmers makes adoption slow
Failure: Lack of funding means limited reach
Failure: Small fragmented farms cannot implement all techniques

mark_scheme.question: State three main sources of air pollution in industrial areas.
mark_scheme_points: Exhaust from factories/chimneys burning fossil fuels
Vehicle emissions/transport of goods
Dust from construction or cement manufacturing

mark_scheme.question: Describe the characteristics of the Makran Coastal region.
mark_scheme_points: Rugged sandstone and shale ridges
Narrow sandy beaches
Cliffs along the coast
Mud volcanoes present
Arid and dry climate

mark_scheme.question: Explain why the River Indus fluctuates in its flow throughout the year.
mark_scheme_points: High flow in summer due to melting snow/glaciers
High flow in late summer (July/August) due to monsoon rains
Low flow in winter due to lack of rain and freezing temperatures in catchment areas
Water extracted by barrages and canals reduces flow downstream

mark_scheme.question: Describe the advantages of establishing Export Processing Zones (EPZs).
mark_scheme_points: Attracts foreign investment (FDI)
Tax holidays/exemptions reduce costs for businesses
Creates employment opportunities
Boosts exports and foreign exchange reserves
Transfer of modern technology to Pakistan

mark_scheme.question: Explain the importance of the cement industry to Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Essential for the construction of housing and infrastructure (dams, roads, bridges)
Uses local raw materials (limestone, gypsum)
Provides employment to thousands
Saves foreign exchange by reducing imports
Can be exported to earn foreign exchange

mark_scheme.question: Describe the distribution of chromite mining in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Mainly in Balochistan (Muslim Bagh, Zhob Valley)
Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (Malakand)
Used locally for making stainless steel

mark_scheme.question: Explain how the government can encourage the development of cottage industries.
mark_scheme_points: Provide microfinance/subsidized loans to poor craftsmen
Establish technical training centers to improve skills
Set up display centers or marketing organizations to sell products locally and abroad
Provide reliable electricity to rural areas to power small machinery

mark_scheme.question: Describe the features of the dry ports in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Located inland, away from seaports
Handle customs clearance locally to save time
Connected by rail or road to major seaports like Karachi/Port Qasim
Provide storage and warehousing facilities
Reduce congestion at main seaports

mark_scheme.question: Explain the problems restricting the growth of the tourism industry in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Security concerns/terrorism discourage international tourists
Lack of basic facilities (clean water, electricity) in remote scenic areas
Poor road infrastructure makes mountainous areas difficult to reach
Lack of international marketing / poor image abroad
Deforestation and pollution damaging the natural beauty of sites

mark_scheme.question: Explain the geographical conditions needed for the growth of rice.
mark_scheme_points: High temperatures (over 20-25°C) during the growing season
Large amounts of water / flooded fields for roots to remain submerged
Heavy rainfall or reliable irrigation (canal network)
Clayey/loamy soil which retains water

mark_scheme.question: Describe the methods used by farmers to prevent soil erosion.
mark_scheme_points: Terracing: cutting steps in slopes to slow down water flow
Contour ploughing: ploughing across slopes instead of up and down
Planting trees/afforestation: roots bind the soil together
Cover crops: leaving vegetation on fields after harvest to protect soil from wind
Building embankments/stone walls to stop surface runoff

mark_scheme.question: Explain the effects of waterlogging and salinity on agriculture.
mark_scheme_points: Waterlogging deprives plant roots of oxygen, killing the plant
Salinity leaves a crust of toxic salt on the surface
Reduces crop yields significantly
Forces farmers to abandon fertile land (turns it barren/thar)

mark_scheme.question: Describe the distribution of the iron and steel industry in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Pakistan Steel Mills located in Karachi (Bin Qasim) due to port access for imported coal/iron ore
Heavy Mechanical Complex located in Taxila
Numerous small-scale re-rolling mills in Punjab (Lahore, Gujranwala)

mark_scheme.question: Explain the importance of the fishing industry to Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Source of protein for local diet
Provides employment to coastal communities (Makran and Sindh coasts)
Earns foreign exchange through the export of high-value fish and shrimp
Used to produce fish meal for poultry feed

mark_scheme.question: Describe the problems of the fishing industry.
mark_scheme_points: Overfishing depletes fish stocks
Water pollution (oil spills, industrial waste from Karachi) kills marine life
Destruction of mangroves removes breeding grounds
Lack of modern trawlers and cold storage facilities limits catch and causes spoilage

mark_scheme.question: Describe the features of a rural settlement in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Small population size
Houses made of mud/kacha bricks or unbaked materials
Agriculture is the primary occupation
Lack of basic amenities like proper hospitals, high schools, and paved roads
Close-knit communities often arranged around a mosque or central well

mark_scheme.question: Explain the causes of rural-to-urban migration.
mark_scheme_points: Push: mechanization in farming means fewer agricultural jobs
Push: lack of good schools, hospitals, and entertainment in villages
Pull: perception of higher wages and regular employment in city factories
Pull: better living standards, electricity, and clean water in cities
Push: natural disasters like floods destroying rural livelihoods

mark_scheme.question: Describe the natural drainage of Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Dominated by the Indus River and its eastern tributaries (Jhelum, Chenab, Ravi, Sutlej)
Rivers generally flow from north to south
Western tributaries include Kabul and Kurram rivers
Inland drainage basins exist in Balochistan (e.g., Hamun-e-Mashkel)
Ends in a large delta before entering the Arabian Sea

mark_scheme.question: Explain the economic importance of the Karachi port.
mark_scheme_points: Handles the majority of Pakistan inland trade / imports and exports
Provides jobs in port operations, warehousing, and shipping
Generates customs and tax revenue for the government
Supports nearby industrial zones (SITE, Korangi) by facilitating raw material import

mark_scheme.question: Describe the distribution of the cotton textile industry.
mark_scheme_points: Concentrated in Faisalabad (the Manchester of Pakistan)
Large presence in Karachi due to port export facilities
Found in Multan and Lahore close to cotton-growing areas in Punjab

mark_scheme.question: Explain why the cotton textile industry is the largest industry in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Abundant local production of raw cotton (the silver fiber)
Huge local market demand for clothing from a large population
Cheap and abundant labor force for spinning and weaving
Government incentives and subsidies for export
Historical expertise and long-established infrastructure in the region

mark_scheme.question: Describe the role of the government in promoting agriculture.
mark_scheme_points: Building and maintaining large dams and canal networks for irrigation
Providing subsidized fertilizers and HYV seeds
Setting up agricultural research institutes (PARC) to develop disease-resistant crops
Offering support prices for major crops (wheat, sugarcane) to guarantee farmer income
Providing loans through the Zarai Taraqiati Bank (ZTBL)

mark_scheme.question: Explain the importance of livestock farming to the rural economy.
mark_scheme_points: Provides milk and meat for family nutrition
Animals are sold for cash during emergencies (acts as an insurance/saving)
Cattle and buffalo are used as draught animals for ploughing fields
Animal dung is used as fertilizer or dried for cooking fuel
Hides and skins can be sold to the leather industry

mark_scheme.question: Describe the physical features of the Potohar Plateau.
mark_scheme_points: Uneven, heavily dissected/faulted land surface
Between 300m to 600m in altitude
Characterized by ravines, gullies, and badland topography due to water erosion
Contains the Salt Range in the south
Contains residual hills like the Khairi Murat
Soils are generally residual and not very fertile

mark_scheme.question: Explain the reasons for the high population growth rate in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Declining death rate due to better medical facilities and vaccination drives
High birth rate continuing due to lack of family planning
Cultural desire for large families to support parents in old age
Religious misunderstandings opposing birth control

mark_scheme.question: Describe the climatic characteristics of the Desert climate zone in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Very low annual precipitation (less than 250mm)
Extremely hot summers with daytime temperatures often exceeding 40°C
High diurnal (daily) temperature range (hot days, cold nights)
Frequent dust storms in summer
Mild to cool winters

mark_scheme.question: Explain why wind power is underdeveloped in Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: High initial capital cost to install wind turbines
Lack of modern technology and skilled engineers to maintain the turbines
Only specific coastal areas (Sindh/Makran) and certain corridors have constant high wind speeds
Government reliance historically on fossil fuels and large hydro projects

mark_scheme.question: Describe the structure of Pakistan inland water transport.
mark_scheme_points: Very limited use of rivers for transport due to seasonal flow variations
Some small boats used on the River Indus in southern Sindh for localized transport
Barrages and dams obstruct continuous navigation along the rivers

mark_scheme.question: Explain the importance of the fertilizer industry to Pakistan economy.
mark_scheme_points: Increases agricultural crop yields on exhausted soils
Reduces the need to import expensive fertilizers, saving foreign exchange
Uses local natural gas as a raw material, adding value
Ensures national food security by boosting wheat and rice production

mark_scheme.question: Describe the problems caused by rapid urbanization.
mark_scheme_points: Development of katchi abadis / squatter settlements with poor sanitation
Traffic congestion and severe air pollution
Overburdening of public services (hospitals, schools, water supply)
Increased crime rates and unemployment in cities

mark_scheme.question: Explain the benefits of improving the national highway network.
mark_scheme_points: Faster transport of agricultural goods to urban markets, reducing spoilage
Better integration of remote provinces (Balochistan, KP) with the economic center
Stimulates industrial growth by linking factories to raw materials and seaports
Encourages the development of businesses along the routes (petrol pumps, motels)

mark_scheme.question: Describe the role of the Trade Development Authority of Pakistan (TDAP) / Export Promotion Bureau.
mark_scheme_points: Organizes international trade fairs and exhibitions for Pakistani products
Provides market intelligence and research to local manufacturers
Helps set quality standards to meet international regulations
Acts as a bridge between the government and exporters

mark_scheme.question: Explain the negative effects of the brain drain on Pakistan.
mark_scheme_points: Loss of highly skilled professionals (doctors, engineers, scientists)
Shortage of experienced personnel hampers the development of local industries
Wasted government investment in subsidized higher education from which other countries benefit
Loss of potential innovative leaders and entrepreneurs
